entry.content:
• The basic principles of probability and counting
    • Basic counting
    • Basic probability
  • Conditional Probability
    • Law of Addition and Multiplication
    • Independence of Events
  • Bayes’s Theorem
    • Law of Total Probability
    • Bayesian Inference
  • Random variables distribution
    • Discrete Random Variable
    • Continuous Random Variable
    • Independence of random variables
  • Expectation and Variance
    • Mean and median
    • Expectation
    • Variance
  • Discrete Probability Distribution
    • Discrete Uniform Distribution
    • Bernoulli and Binomial Distribution
    • Categorical and Multipomial Distribution
    • Hypergeometric Distribution
    • Poisson Distribution
  • Continuous Probability Distribution
    • Continuous Uniform Distribution
    • Gaussian Distribution
    • Exponential Distribution
    • Gamma Distribution
    • Beta Distribution
  • Recap of basic probability
    • Four fundamental concepts in probability
    • Bayesian reasoning
    • Random variable
    • Table of Distributions

The basic principles of probability and counting

Basic counting

  • Permutation:

    The permutation indicates how many different ordered arrangements can be made from a set of nnn items, calculated by:

    P(n)=n!\begin{equation} P(n) = n! \end{equation}P(n)=n!​​

  • Combination:

    The combination indicates how many ways to select kkk items from a set of nnn items (k<nk < nk<n) without order arrangement, calculated by:

    (nk)=C(n, k)=n!k!(n−k)!\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \begin{pmatrix}n\\ k\end{pmatrix} = C(n,\ k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} \end{aligned} \end{equation}(nk​)=C(n, k)=k!(n−k)!n!​​​​

    • For any positive nnn and kkk with k≤nk \leq nk≤n, we also have:

      (nk)=(nn−k)\begin{aligned} \begin{pmatrix}n\\ k\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}n\\ n - k\end{pmatrix} \end{aligned}(nk​)=(nn−k​)​

      and

      k(nk)=n(n−1k−1)\begin{aligned} k\begin{pmatrix}n\\ k\end{pmatrix} = n\begin{pmatrix}n-1\\ k-1\end{pmatrix} \end{aligned}k(nk​)=n(n−1k−1​)​

  • kkk-permutations of nnn:

    The kkk-permutation of nnn indicates many ways to select kkk items from a set of nnn items (k<nk < nk<n) with order arrangement, calculated by:

    P(n, k)=n!(n−k)!\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} P(n,\ k) = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} \end{aligned} \end{equation}P(n, k)=(n−k)!n!​​​​

  • The binomial theorem:

    (x+y)n=∑k=0n(nk)xkyn−k\begin{equation} (x + y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^n \begin{pmatrix}n\\ k\end{pmatrix}x^ky^{n-k} \end{equation}(x+y)n=k=0∑n​(nk​)xkyn−k​​

Basic probability

  • Given two events AAA and BBB, we have:
    • A∪BA\cup BA∪B is the event that occurs if at least one of AAA or BBB occurs
    • A∩BA\cap BA∩B is the event that occurs if both AAA and BBB occurs
    • The complement AcA^cAc is the event that occurs if and only if AAA does not occur

  • De Morgan’s Law:

    (A∪B)c=Ac∩Bc(A∩B)c=Ac∪Bc\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} (A\cup B)^c =A^c \cap B^c \\ \\ (A\cap B)^c =A^c \cup B^c \end{aligned} \end{equation}(A∪B)c=Ac∩Bc(A∩B)c=Ac∪Bc​​​

    • General form:

      Given a set of events A1, A2,⋯ , AnA_1, \ A_2, \cdots, \ A_nA1​, A2​,⋯, An​, we have:

      (⋃i=1nAi)c=⋂i=1nAi\begin{aligned} \left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i\right)^c = \bigcap_{i=1}^n A_i \end{aligned}(i=1⋃n​Ai​)c=i=1⋂n​Ai​​ (⋂i=1nAi)c=⋃i=1nAi\begin{aligned} \left(\bigcap_{i=1}^n A_i\right)^c = \bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i \end{aligned}(i=1⋂n​Ai​)c=i=1⋃n​Ai​​

  • Naive definition of probability:

    Pnaive(A)=∣A∣∣S∣=number of outcomes favorable to Atotal number of outcomes in SP_\text{naive}(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|} = \frac{\text{number of outcomes favorable to }A}{\text{total number of outcomes in }S}Pnaive​(A)=∣S∣∣A∣​=total number of outcomes in Snumber of outcomes favorable to A​

  • General definition of probability:

    A probability space consists of a sample space SSS and a probability function PPP which takes an event. A⊆SA \subseteq SA⊆S (AAA is the subset of SSS) as input and returns P(A)P(A)P(A), a real number between 000 and 111, as output. The function PPP must satisfy the following axioms:

    • P(∅)=0,P(S)=1P(\emptyset) = 0, \qquad P(S) = 1P(∅)=0,P(S)=1

    • If A1, A2,⋯A_1, \ A_2, \cdotsA1​, A2​,⋯ are disjoint events (mutually exclusive: Ai∩Aj=∅A_i \cap A_j = \emptysetAi​∩Aj​=∅ for i≠ji \neq ji=j), then

      P(⋃j=1∞Aj)=∑j=1∞P(Aj)P\left(\bigcup_{j=1}^\infty A_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^\infty P(A_j)P(j=1⋃∞​Aj​)=j=1∑∞​P(Aj​)

Conditional Probability

  • With two events A and B, the probability of AAA given that the event BBB already happened is the “conditional probability of A given B” P(A∣B)P(A\mid B)P(A∣B), calculated by:

    P(A∣B)=P(A∩B)P(B)\begin{equation} P(A\mid B) = \frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)} \end{equation}P(A∣B)=P(B)P(A∩B)​​​
    • In general: P(A∣B)≠P(B∣A)P(A \mid B) ≠ P(B \mid A)P(A∣B)=P(B∣A) when P(A)≠P(B)P(A) ≠ P(B)P(A)=P(B)

  • Example:

    • In August, the probability of a rainy day is 0.80.80.8. The probability there is a rainy day with clouds in the sky is 0.60.60.6. Thus, the probability that there is a rainy day when the sky cloud is:
    P(rain∣cloud)=P(rain ∩ cloud)P(cloud)=0.60.8=0.75P(rain \mid cloud) = \frac{P(rain \ \cap \ cloud)}{P(cloud)} = \frac{0.6}{0.8} = 0.75P(rain∣cloud)=P(cloud)P(rain ∩ cloud)​=0.80.6​=0.75

Law of Addition and Multiplication

  • Multiplication rule: The joint probability of having both AAA and BBB occur is:

    P(A∩B)=P(A, B)=P(A∣B)⋅P(B)\begin{equation} P(A\cap B) = P(A, \ B) = P(A\mid B)\cdot P(B) \end{equation}P(A∩B)=P(A, B)=P(A∣B)⋅P(B)​​

  • Additional rule: The probability that two events AAA or BBB occur is:

    P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)\begin{equation} P(A\cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B) \end{equation}P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)​​

  • Special cases:

    • When the AAA and BBB are mutually exclusive (two events cannot happen at the same time):
    P(A∩B)=0P(A\cap B) = 0P(A∩B)=0 P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B)
    • When AAA and BBB are not mutually exclusive and independent:
    P(A∩B)=P(A)⋅P(B)P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)P(A∩B)=P(A)⋅P(B)

    • The cases with 3 events AAA, BBB, and CCC:

      P(A,B,C)=P(A)⋅P(B∣A)⋅P(C∣A,B)P(A∪B∪B)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)−P(A∩B)−P(A∩C)−P(B∩C)+P(A∩B∩C)\begin{aligned} P(A,B,C) &= P(A)\cdot P(B \mid A) \cdot P(C\mid A, B) \\ P(A \cup B \cup B) &= P(A) + P(B) + P(C) \\ & \quad - P(A \cap B) - P(A \cap C) - P(B \cap C) \\ & \quad + P(A \cap B \cap C) \end{aligned}P(A,B,C)P(A∪B∪B)​=P(A)⋅P(B∣A)⋅P(C∣A,B)=P(A)+P(B)+P(C)−P(A∩B)−P(A∩C)−P(B∩C)+P(A∩B∩C)​

Independence of Events

  • Independent events

    • AAA and BBB are two independent events if:

      P(A∩B)=P(A)⋅P(B)P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)P(A∩B)=P(A)⋅P(B)

    • If P(A)>0P(A) >0P(A)>0 and P(B)>0P(B)> 0P(B)>0, then:

      P(A∣B)=P(A)P(A\mid B) = P(A)P(A∣B)=P(A) P(B∣A)=P(B)P(B\mid A) = P(B)P(B∣A)=P(B)

    • If AAA and BBB are independent, then AAA and BcB^cBc are independent, AcA^cAc and BBB are independent, and AcA^cAc and BcB^cBc are independent.

  • Conditional independent:

    • Assume that AAA, BBB, and CCC are three events. We say that events A and B are independent given event C if:

      P(A,B∣C)=P(A∣C)⋅P(B∣C)\begin{equation} P(A, B \mid C) = P(A \mid C) \cdot P(B \mid C) \end{equation}P(A,B∣C)=P(A∣C)⋅P(B∣C)​​
      • This notion means that once we know event CCC, the events AAA and BBB do not convey information about each other

    • “Independence” ≠ “Conditional independence”

      • “Event A and B are independent” does not mean that “A and B are independence given event C”

      • Events A and B can be dependent and yet they are independent given an event C

Bayes’s Theorem

  • With Bayes’s Theorem, P(A∣B)P(A \mid B)P(A∣B) can be calculated by:

    P(A∣B)=P(B∣A)⋅P(A)P(B)\begin{equation} P(A\mid B) = \frac{P(B|A)\cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \end{equation}P(A∣B)=P(B)P(B∣A)⋅P(A)​​​
    • Notations:
      • P(A)P(A)P(A): the prior probability, namely, how probable the event AAA is before we have seen the data information BBB
      • P(B∣A)P(B \mid A)P(B∣A): the likelihood, namely, conditional on event AAA, how likely is that we would see data information BBB
      • P(B)P(B)P(B): the marginal (unconditional) probability of BBB (regardless of whether event AAA holds or not)
      • P(A∣B)P(A\mid B)P(A∣B): the posterior probability, namely, how probable the event AAA after we have seen the information BBB
  • Variants of Bayes’s Theorem:

    • Odd form:

      P(A∣B)P(Ac∣B)=P(B∣A)P(B∣Ac)⋅P(A)P(Ac)\frac{P(A \mid B)}{P(A^c \mid B)} = \frac{P(B \mid A)}{P(B \mid A^c)} \cdot \frac{P(A)}{P(A^c)}P(Ac∣B)P(A∣B)​=P(B∣Ac)P(B∣A)​⋅P(Ac)P(A)​

      where AcA^cAc is the event that AAA does not hold

    • With 3 events A, B, CA, \ B, \ CA, B, C, we have:

      P(A∣B,C)=P(B∣A,C)⋅P(A∣C)P(B∣C)=P(A,B,C)P(B∣C)=P(B,C∣A)⋅P(A)P(B,C)\begin{aligned} P(A \mid B, C) &= \frac{P(B \mid A, C) \cdot P(A \mid C)}{P(B \mid C)} \\&= \frac{P(A,B,C)}{P(B \mid C)} = \frac{P(B, C\mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B, C)} \end{aligned}P(A∣B,C)​=P(B∣C)P(B∣A,C)⋅P(A∣C)​=P(B∣C)P(A,B,C)​=P(B,C)P(B,C∣A)⋅P(A)​​

  • Understand better Bayes’s theorem:

    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HZGCoVF3YvM

  • Examples:

    • In September, the probability of a rainy day is 0.60.60.6. The probability there is a cloudy day is 0.30.30.3. The probability that there are clouds on a rainy day is 0.40.40.4. Therefore, the probability that there is a rainy day when the sky cloud is:

      P(rain∣cloud)=P(rain)⋅P(cloud∣rain)P(cloud)=0.6×0.40.3=0.8P(rain \mid cloud) = \frac{P(rain) \cdot P(cloud \mid rain)}{P(cloud)} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.4}{0.3} = 0.8P(rain∣cloud)=P(cloud)P(rain)⋅P(cloud∣rain)​=0.30.6×0.4​=0.8

    • A particular test when someone has been using cannabis is 90%90\%90% sensitive, leading to the true positive rate being 0.90.90.9, the test is also 80%80\%80% specific, meaning the true negative rate is 0.80.80.8. Therefore, the test correctly identifies 80%80\%80% of non-user as non-user. Assuming for a group of people, there are 5%5\%5% that use cannabis, which is 0.50.50.5 prevalence. The possibility that random people test correctly as a cannabis user is:

      P(User∣Positive)=P(Positive∣User)⋅P(User)P(Positive)=0.9×0.050.08=0.19P(User \mid Positive) = \frac{P(Positive\mid User)\cdot P(User)}{P(Positive)} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.05}{0.08} = 0.19P(User∣Positive)=P(Positive)P(Positive∣User)⋅P(User)​=0.080.9×0.05​=0.19

Law of Total Probability

  • Ω\OmegaΩ denotes the set of all possible outcomes

  • Given an event space:

    Ω=B1∪B2∪B3∪⋯∪Bm\Omega = B_1 \cup B_2 \cup B_3 \cup \cdots \cup B_mΩ=B1​∪B2​∪B3​∪⋯∪Bm​

    with an arbitrary event B∈ΩB \in \OmegaB∈Ω, we have the Law of Total Probability:

    P(B)=∑i=1mP(B∩Ai)=∑i=1mP(B∣Ai)⋅P(Ai)\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} P(B) &= \sum_{i=1}^mP(B\cap A_i) \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^mP(B\mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i) \end{aligned} \end{equation}P(B)​=i=1∑m​P(B∩Ai​)=i=1∑m​P(B∣Ai​)⋅P(Ai​)​​​

  • Simpson’s Paradox

    • Senior doctors take 213 easy cases, 102 hard cases, a total of 315 cases. While the junior doctors take 3375 cases including 3169 easy cases and 206 hard cases. Explain the paradox:

      • With AAA = {complication rates of senior doctors}

        P(A)=P(A∣easy)⋅P(easy)+P(A∣hard)⋅P(hard)=0.052×213315+0.127×102315=0.076\begin{aligned} P(A) &= P(A \mid easy) \cdot P(easy) + P(A \mid hard) \cdot P(hard) \\ &= 0.052 \times \frac{213}{315} + 0.127 \times \frac{102}{315} = 0.076 \end{aligned}P(A)​=P(A∣easy)⋅P(easy)+P(A∣hard)⋅P(hard)=0.052×315213​+0.127×315102​=0.076​

      • With BBB = {complication rates of junior doctors}

        P(B)=P(B∣easy)⋅P(easy)+P(B∣hard)⋅P(hard)=0.067×31693375+0.155×2063375=0.072\begin{aligned} P(B) &= P(B \mid easy) \cdot P(easy) + P(B \mid hard) \cdot P(hard) \\ &= 0.067 \times \frac{3169}{3375} + 0.155 \times \frac{206}{3375} = 0.072 \end{aligned}P(B)​=P(B∣easy)⋅P(easy)+P(B∣hard)⋅P(hard)=0.067×33753169​+0.155×3375206​=0.072​

      • Thus, we observe that the reason for Simpson’s paradox is:

        • The junior doctors mostly perform easy cases with low complication rates
        • The senior doctors have a higher fraction of hard cases, which leads to a higher overall rate
    • Therefore, it is possible to have
    P(A∣B,C)<P(A∣Bc,C)andP(A∣B,Cc)<P(A∣Bc,Cc)yet P(A∣B)>P(A∣Bc)\begin{aligned} &P(A|B,C) < P(A|B^c, C) \qquad \text{and} \qquad P(A|B,C^c) < P(A|B^c, C^c) \\ \text{yet} \qquad & \qquad \ \quad \qquad \qquad \qquad P(A|B) > P(A|B^c) \end{aligned}yet​P(A∣B,C)<P(A∣Bc,C)andP(A∣B,Cc)<P(A∣Bc,Cc) P(A∣B)>P(A∣Bc)​

  • In the standard case with two events AAA, BBB, with the Law of Total Probability, P(B)P(B)P(B) can be calculated by:

    P(B)=P(B∣A)⋅P(A)+P(B∣Ac)⋅P(Ac)\begin{equation} P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B|A^c) \cdot P(A^c) \end{equation}P(B)=P(B∣A)⋅P(A)+P(B∣Ac)⋅P(Ac)​​
    • Example:
      • 50%50\%50% of emails are spam. A certain software can detect 99%99\%99% of spam emails, with the probability that non-spam email is detected as spam email is 5%5\%5%. If an email is detected as spam email, what is the probability that this is in fact a non-spam email?
      • Initially, we have:
      P(A)=P(Ac)=0.5P(A) = P(A^c) = 0.5P(A)=P(Ac)=0.5 P(B∣Ac)=0.99P(B \mid A^c) = 0.99P(B∣Ac)=0.99 P(B∣A)=0.05P(B | A) = 0.05P(B∣A)=0.05
      • By the law of total probability:
      P(B)=P(B∣A)⋅P(A)+P(B∣Ac)⋅P(Ac)=0.05×0.5+0.99×0.5=0.52\begin{aligned} P(B) &= P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B|A^c) \cdot P(A^c)\\ &= 0.05 \times 0.5 + 0.99 \times 0.5 = 0.52 \end{aligned}P(B)​=P(B∣A)⋅P(A)+P(B∣Ac)⋅P(Ac)=0.05×0.5+0.99×0.5=0.52​

      • With Bayes’s Theorem:

        P(A∣B)=0.05×0.50.52≈0.048=4.8%P(A \mid B) = \frac{0.05 \times 0.5}{0.52} \approx 0.048 = 4.8\%P(A∣B)=0.520.05×0.5​≈0.048=4.8%

Bayesian Inference

  • Bayesian inference is a method of statistical inference in which Bayes’s Theorem is used to update the probability of a hypothesis as more data become available:

    Or:

    Posterior= Likelihood× Prior ÷ EvidencePosterior = Likelihood × Prior ÷ EvidencePosterior= Likelihood× Prior ÷ Evidence

Random variables distribution

Given an experiment with sample space Ω\OmegaΩ, a random variable(s) (r.v.) is a measurable function X:S→EX:S → EX:S→E from a set of possible outcomes S∈ΩS \in \OmegaS∈Ω to the real number E∈RE \in \mathbb{R}E∈R

Discrete Random Variable

  • A random variable that can take on at most a countable number of possible values (either finite or countably infinity) is said to be a discrete random variable.

  • For a discrete random variable XXX, the probability mass function (p.m.f.) of XXX is the function p(a)p(a)p(a) gives the probability that a XXX takes on the value aaa:

    p(a)=P(X=a)\begin{equation} p(a) = P(X = a) \end{equation}p(a)=P(X=a)​​
    • If XXX is a discrete r.s., then the finite or countably infinity set of value xxx such that P(X=x)>0P(X = x) > 0P(X=x)>0 is called the support of x

  • The cumulative distribution function (c.d.f.) of a random variables XXX (or more simply, the distribution function of XXX) is the function FX(a)F_X(a)FX​(a) given by:

    FX(a)=P(X≤a)=∑X≤a p(X)−∞<x<∞\begin{equation} F_X(a) = P(X \le a)= \underset{X \le a}{\sum}\ p(X) \qquad \qquad -\infty < x < \infty \end{equation}FX​(a)=P(X≤a)=X≤a∑​ p(X)−∞<x<∞​​
    • We can write FX(a)F_X(a)FX​(a) by F(a)F(a)F(a) for convenience if there is no risk of ambiguity
    • c.d.f. is defined for all random variables, regardless it is discrete or continuous
  • Examples:

    • Example 1: Suppose that we have an experiment of tossing the coin 3 times. Then we have

      Ω={HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT}\Omega = \{HHH, \ HHT, \ HTH, \ HTT, \ THH, \ THT,\ TTH, \ TTT \}Ω={HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT}.

      If we let XXX denote the number of heads that appear, then XXX is a discrete random variable that takes one of the values E={0,..,3}E =\{0,..,3\}E={0,..,3}.

      • Thus, we have the p.m.f. of XXX with the respective probability:
      p(0)=P(X=0)=P({TTT})=18p(1)=P(X=1)=P({TTH, THT, HTT})=38p(2)=P(X=2)=P({THH, HTH, HHT})=38p(3)=P(X=3)=P({HHH})=18\begin{aligned} &p(0) = P(X = 0) = P(\{TTT\}) = \frac{1}{8}\\ &p(1) = P(X = 1) = P(\{TTH, \ THT, \ HTT\}) = \frac{3}{8}\\ &p(2) = P(X = 2) = P(\{THH, \ HTH, \ HHT\}) = \frac{3}{8}\\ &p(3) = P(X = 3) = P(\{HHH\}) = \frac{1}{8}\\ \end{aligned}​p(0)=P(X=0)=P({TTT})=81​p(1)=P(X=1)=P({TTH, THT, HTT})=83​p(2)=P(X=2)=P({THH, HTH, HHT})=83​p(3)=P(X=3)=P({HHH})=81​​
      • Given a=2a = 2a=2, the c.d.f. of F(a)F(a)F(a) with the respective probability is given by:
      F(2)=P(X≤2)=p(0)+p(1)+p(2)=78\begin{aligned} F(2) = P(X \le 2) = p(0) + p(1) + p(2) = \frac{7}{8} \end{aligned}F(2)=P(X≤2)=p(0)+p(1)+p(2)=87​​

    • Example 2: The p.m.f. of a random variable XXX is given by p(i)=cλii!, i=0,1,2,⋯ ,p(i) = c \frac{\lambda^i}{i!}, \ i = 0, 1, 2,\cdots,p(i)=ci!λi​, i=0,1,2,⋯, where λ\lambdaλ is some positive value. Find (a) P(X=0)P(X=0)P(X=0) and F(3)F(3)F(3):

      • Since ∑i=0∞p(i)=1\sum_{i=0}^\infty p(i) = 1∑i=0∞​p(i)=1, we have:

        c∑i=0∞λii!=1c\sum_{i=0}^\infty\frac{\lambda^i}{i!} = 1ci=0∑∞​i!λi​=1

        however, because ex=∑i=0∞λii!e^x = \sum_{i=0}^\infty\frac{\lambda^i}{i!}ex=∑i=0∞​i!λi​, then we have:

        ceλ=1⇒c=e−λce^\lambda = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = e^{-\lambda}ceλ=1⇒c=e−λ

      • Hence: P(X=0)=e−λλ00!=e−λP(X = 0) = e^{-\lambda}\frac{\lambda^0}{0!} = e^{-\lambda}P(X=0)=e−λ0!λ0​=e−λ

        F(3)=∑X≤3P(X)=P(0)+P(1)+P(2)+P(3)=e−λ+λe−λ+λ2e−λ2+λ3e−λ6=λ3+3λ2+6λ+66e−λ\begin{aligned} F(3) &= \sum_{X \leq 3}P(X) = P(0) + P(1) + P(2) + P(3)\\ &= e^{-\lambda} + \lambda e^{-\lambda} + \frac{\lambda^2 e^{-\lambda}}{2} + \frac{\lambda^3 e^{-\lambda}}{6} \\ &= \frac{\lambda^3 + 3 \lambda^2 + 6 \lambda + 6}{6}e^{-\lambda} \end{aligned}F(3)​=X≤3∑​P(X)=P(0)+P(1)+P(2)+P(3)=e−λ+λe−λ+2λ2e−λ​+6λ3e−λ​=6λ3+3λ2+6λ+6​e−λ​

Continuous Random Variable

  • A random variable that has an uncountable number of possible values is said to be a continuous random variable. A random variable is continuous if its c.d.f. is differentiable (can be derivative at every point in its domain). Also, it must have a differentiable distribution.

  • For a continuous r.v. XXX with c.d.f. FFF, the probability density function (p.d.f.) f(x)f(x)f(x) of XXX is the derivative of FFF, given by f(x)=F’(x)f(x) = F’(x)f(x)=F’(x). Thus, the p.d.f. f(x)f(x)f(x) can be defined for all real x∈(−∞, ∞)x \in (- \infty, \ \infty)x∈(−∞, ∞), having the property that, for any set BBB of real numbers:

    P(X∈B)=∫Bf(x) dx\begin{equation} P(X \in B) = \int_B f(x)\ dx \end{equation}P(X∈B)=∫B​f(x) dx​​
    • The p.d.f. fff of a continuous random variable must satisfy the following two criteria:
      • Nonnegative: f(x)≥0f(x) \ge 0f(x)≥0
      • Integrates to 111: ∫−∞∞f(x) dx=1\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \ dx = 1∫−∞∞​f(x) dx=1
    • The support of X, and of its distribution, is the set of all xxx where f(x)>0f(x) > 0f(x)>0.

  • Special cases:

    P(X=a)=∫aaf(x) dx=0P(a≤X≤b)=∫abf(x) dx\begin{aligned} &P\left(X = a\right) = \int_{a}^{a} f(x) \ dx = 0 \\ &P\left(a \le X \le b\right) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \ dx \end{aligned}​P(X=a)=∫aa​f(x) dx=0P(a≤X≤b)=∫ab​f(x) dx​

  • p.d.f. to c.d.f.: The c.d.f. F(a)F(a)F(a) of continuous r.v. XXX given the p.d.f. f(x)f(x)f(x) is calculated by:

    F(a)=P(X≤a)=∫−∞af(x) dx\begin{equation} F(a) = P(X \le a) = \int_{-\infty}^a f(x)\ dx \end{equation}F(a)=P(X≤a)=∫−∞a​f(x) dx​​

  • Examples:

    • Example 1: The amount of time in hours that a computer functions before breaking down is a continuous random variable XXX with the probability density function given by:

      f(x)={C(4x−2x2)0<x<20otherwise\begin{aligned} f(x) = \begin{cases} C(4x - 2 x^2) & 0 < x < 2 \\ 0 &\text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned}f(x)={C(4x−2x2)0​0<x<2otherwise​​

      What is the value of CCC and calculate P(X>1)P(X > 1)P(X>1)?

      Solution:

      • Since f(x)f(x)f(x) is a p.d.f., we must have:

        ∫−∞∞f(x) dx=1⇔ C∫−02(4x−2x2) dx=1⇔ C[2x2−2x33]∣x=0x=2=1⇒ C=38\begin{aligned} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \ dx &= 1 \\ \Leftrightarrow \ C\int_{-0}^{2} (4x - 2x^2) \ dx &= 1 \\ \Leftrightarrow \ C\left[2x^2 - \frac{2x^3}{3}\right]\Bigg|_{x=0}^{x=2} &= 1 \\ \Rightarrow \ C &= \frac{3}{8} \end{aligned}∫−∞∞​f(x) dx⇔ C∫−02​(4x−2x2) dx⇔ C[2x2−32x3​]∣∣​x=0x=2​⇒ C​=1=1=1=83​​

      • Integrate the p.d.f. from 111 to +∞+ \infty+∞, and we have:

        P(X>1)=∫1∞f(x) dx=38∫12(4x−2x2) dx=12\begin{aligned} P(X > 1) = \int_1^\infty f(x) \ dx = \frac{3}{8} \int_1^2(4x-2x^2) \ dx = \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}P(X>1)=∫1∞​f(x) dx=83​∫12​(4x−2x2) dx=21​​

    • Example 2: A continuous random variable XXX follows the Logistic distribution with the following c.d.f.:

      F(X)=ex1+ex,x∈R\begin{aligned} F(X) = \frac{e^x}{1 + e^x}, \qquad x \in \mathbb{R} \end{aligned}F(X)=1+exex​,x∈R​

      Find the p.d.f. f(x)f(x)f(x) and P(−2<X<2)P(-2 < X < 2)P(−2<X<2):

      Solution:

      • To obtain the p.d.f., we differentiate the c.d.f., which gives:

        f(x)=F(X)′=ddx(ex1+ex)=ex(1+ex)2\begin{aligned} f(x) = F(X)' = \frac{d}{dx} \left(\frac{e^x}{1 + e^x}\right) = \frac{e^x}{(1 + e^x)^2} \end{aligned}f(x)=F(X)′=dxd​(1+exex​)=(1+ex)2ex​​

      • Integrate the p.d.f. from −2-2−2 to 222, and we have:

        P(−2<X<2)=∫−22ex(1+ex)2 dx=F(2)−F(−2)≈0.76\begin{aligned} P(-2 < X < 2) = \int_{-2}^2\frac{e^x}{(1+e^x)^2} \ dx = F(2) - F(-2) \approx 0.76 \end{aligned}P(−2<X<2)=∫−22​(1+ex)2ex​ dx=F(2)−F(−2)≈0.76​

Independence of random variables

  • If two random variables XXX and YYY are independent, then knowing the value of XXX gives no information about the value of YYY, and vice versa.

  • Therefore, two random variables XXX and YYY are independent for all x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R if:

    FX,Y(x,y)=FX(x)⋅FY(y)for all x,y\begin{equation} F_{X, Y}(x, y) = F_X(x)\cdot F_Y(y) \qquad \text{for all} \ x, y \end{equation}FX,Y​(x,y)=FX​(x)⋅FY​(y)for all x,y​​

    • Equivalently, in the discrete case, given the p.m.f. P(X=x)P(X=x)P(X=x) and P(Y=y)P(Y = y)P(Y=y) and the joint probability mass function P(X=x,Y=y)P(X = x, Y = y)P(X=x,Y=y) exist:

      P(X=x,Y=y)=P(X=x)⋅P(Y=y)P(X = x, Y = y) = P(X=x)\cdot P(Y=y)P(X=x,Y=y)=P(X=x)⋅P(Y=y)

    • Or equivalently, in the continuous case, given the p.d.f. fX(x)f_X(x)fX​(x) and fY(y)f_Y(y)fY​(y) and the joint probability density functyion fX,Y(x,y)f_{X,Y}(x,y)fX,Y​(x,y) exist:

      fX,Y(x,y)=fX(x)⋅fY(y)f_{X, Y}(x, y) = f_X(x)\cdot f_Y(y)fX,Y​(x,y)=fX​(x)⋅fY​(y)

  • Two random variables XXX and YYY are conditionally independent given a random variable ZZZ for all x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R and all zzz in the support of ZZZ if:

    FX,Y∣Z=z(x,y)=FX∣Z=z(x)⋅FY∣Z=z(y)for all  x,y,z\begin{equation} F_{X, Y \mid Z=z}(x, y) = F_{X \mid Z = z}(x) \cdot F_{Y\mid Z = z}(y) \qquad \text{for all } \ x, y, z \end{equation}FX,Y∣Z=z​(x,y)=FX∣Z=z​(x)⋅FY∣Z=z​(y)for all  x,y,z​​

    • Equivalently, in the discrete case, for any x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R and z∈Rz \in \mathbb{R}z∈R with the p.m.f. P(Z=z)>0P(Z=z) > 0P(Z=z)>0:

      P(X=x,Y=y∣Z=z)=P(X=x∣Z=z)⋅P(Y=y∣Z=z)P(X = x, Y = y | Z = z) = P(X=x | Z=z)\cdot P(Y=y|Z=z)P(X=x,Y=y∣Z=z)=P(X=x∣Z=z)⋅P(Y=y∣Z=z)

    • Or equivalently, in the continuous case, for all x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R and z∈Rz \in \mathbb{R}z∈R with the p.d.f. fZ(z)>0f_Z(z) >0fZ​(z)>0:

      fX,Y∣Z(x,y∣z)=fX∣Z(x∣z)⋅fY∣Z(y∣z)f_{X, Y \mid Z}(x, y|z) = f_{X\mid Z}(x|z)\cdot f_{Y\mid Z}(y|z)fX,Y∣Z​(x,y∣z)=fX∣Z​(x∣z)⋅fY∣Z​(y∣z)
  • Examples: A man and a woman decide to meet at a certain location. If each of them independently arrives at the time uniformly distributed between 12:00 and 13.00, find the probability that the first to arrive has to wait longer than 10 minutes.

    • Denote XXX and YYY are, respectively, the time past 12:00 that the man and the women arrive. Thus, XXX and YYY are two independent random variables, each of which is uniformly distributed over (0, 60)(0, \ 60)(0, 60). We have the desired probability. P(X+10<Y)+P(Y+10<X)=2P(X+1<Y)P(X + 10 < Y) + P(Y + 10 < X) = 2P(X + 1 < Y)P(X+10<Y)+P(Y+10<X)=2P(X+1<Y) (by symmetry), calculated as follows:

      2P(X+10<Y)=∫∫x+10<yf(x,y)dx dy=2∫∫x+10<yfX(x)fY(y)dx dy=2∫1060∫0y−10(160)2dx dy=2360∫1060(y−10)dy=2536\begin{aligned} 2P(X + 10 < Y) &= \underset{x + 10 < y}{\int\int}f(x,y)dx \ dy\\ &= 2\underset{x + 10 < y}{\int\int}f_X(x)f_Y(y)dx \ dy\\ &= 2\int_10^60\int_0^{y-10}\left(\frac{1}{60}\right)^2 dx \ dy \\ &= \frac{2}{360}\int_10^60(y-10)dy\\ &= \frac{25}{36} \end{aligned}2P(X+10<Y)​=x+10<y∫∫​f(x,y)dx dy=2x+10<y∫∫​fX​(x)fY​(y)dx dy=2∫1​060∫0y−10​(601​)2dx dy=3602​∫1​060(y−10)dy=3625​​

Expectation and Variance

Mean and median

  • Given a list of number x1, x2,⋯ ,xnx_1, \ x_2, \cdots, x_nx1​, x2​,⋯,xn​, the arithmetic mean xˉ\bar{x}xˉ is defined by:
xˉ=1n∑j=1nxj\begin{equation} \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^nx_j \end{equation}xˉ=n1​j=1∑n​xj​​​

  • More generally, given the weight p1, p2,⋯ ,pnp_1, \ p_2, \cdots, p_np1​, p2​,⋯,pn​ that add up to 1, the weighted mean (or mean as weighted sum) of x1, x2,⋯ ,xnx_1, \ x_2, \cdots, x_nx1​, x2​,⋯,xn​ is:

    weighted-mean(x)=∑j=1npj⋅xj\begin{equation} \text{weighted-mean}(x) = \sum_{j=1}^np_j\cdot x_j \end{equation}weighted-mean(x)=j=1∑n​pj​⋅xj​​​

  • The median is the value that separates the first half and the second half of a sorted data sample, a population, or probabilistic distribution. It also may be considered “the middle” value. Given a list of sorted numbers x1, x2,⋯ ,xnx_1, \ x_2, \cdots, x_nx1​, x2​,⋯,xn​, there are two ways to find the median:

    • If nnn odd:

      median(x)=x(n+1)/2\text{median}(x) = x_{(n+1)/2}median(x)=x(n+1)/2​

    • If nnn even:

      median(x)=x(n/2)+x(n+1)/22\text{median}(x) = \frac{x_{(n/2)}+x_{(n+1)/2}}{2}median(x)=2x(n/2)​+x(n+1)/2​​

Expectation

The expectation (or expected value) indicates the center value of the distribution of a random variable.

  • Discrete random variable:

    Given XXX is a discrete random variable having a p.m.f. p(x)p(x)p(x), then the expectation of XXX, denoted as E[X]E[X]E[X] (or μ\muμ), is defined by:

    μ=E[X]=∑x:p(x)>0x⋅p(x)\begin{equation} \mu = E[X] = \sum_{x:p(x)>0}x\cdot p(x) \end{equation}μ=E[X]=x:p(x)>0∑​x⋅p(x)​​

    Or it can also be written, if distinct possible values of XXX are x1, x2,⋯ ,xnx_1, \ x_2, \cdots, x_nx1​, x2​,⋯,xn​, by:

    E(X)=∑j=1nxj⋅P(X=xj)E(X) = \sum_{j = 1}^nx_j\cdot P(X=x_j)E(X)=j=1∑n​xj​⋅P(X=xj​)
    • Some theorems:

      • If two random variables XXX and YYY share the same distribution, then:

        E[X]=E[Y]E[X] = E[Y]E[X]=E[Y]

      • For any random variables X, YX, \ YX, Y , and any constant ccc, we have:

        E[X+Y]=E[X]+E[Y]E[cX]=c⋅E[X]\begin{aligned} E[X + Y] &= E[X] + E[Y] \\ E[cX] &= c \cdot E[X] \end{aligned}E[X+Y]E[cX]​=E[X]+E[Y]=c⋅E[X]​

      • Example: A school class of 120 students is driven in 3 buses to a symphonic performance. There are 36 students on one of the buses, 40 on another, and 44 on the third bus. When the buses arrive, one of the 120 is randomly chosen. Let XXX denote the number of students on the bus that are randomly chosen. Find the expectation of XXX.

        • Since the randomly chosen student is equally likely to be any of 120 students, it follows that: P(X=36)=36120;P(X=40)=40120;P(X=44)=44120P(X = 36) = \frac{36}{120}; \quad P(X = 40) = \frac{40}{120}; \quad P(X = 44) = \frac{44}{120}P(X=36)=12036​;P(X=40)=12040​;P(X=44)=12044​

        Thus, we have:

        E[X]=36×310+40×13+44×1130=120830=40.2667E[X] = 36\times\frac{3}{10} + 40\times\frac{1}{3} + 44\times\frac{11}{30} = \frac{1208}{30} = 40.2667E[X]=36×103​+40×31​+44×3011​=301208​=40.2667
    • Law of the unconscious statistician (LOTUS) for discrete r.v.:
      • A function of a random variable is also a random variable. The Law of the unconscious statistician (LOTUS) is a theorem to calculate the expected value of a function g(X)g(X)g(X) of a random variable XXX given the probabilistic distribution of XXX but not g(X)g(X)g(X).

      • If XXX is a discrete random variable that takes on one of the values xi, i≥1x_i, \ i \geq 1xi​, i≥1, with respective probabilities p(xi)p(x_i)p(xi​), then, for any real-valued function ggg from R\mathbb{R}R to R\mathbb{R}R:

        E[g(X)]=∑ig(xi)P(X=xi)=∑ig(xi)p(xi)\begin{equation} E[g(X)] = \sum_i g(x_i)P(X = x_i) = \sum_i g(x_i)p(x_i) \end{equation}E[g(X)]=i∑​g(xi​)P(X=xi​)=i∑​g(xi​)p(xi​)​​
      • Example: Let XXX denote a random variable that takes on any of the values −1, 0, and 1-1, \ 0, \ \text{and} \ 1−1, 0, and 1 with respective probability: P(X=−1)=0.2; P(X=0)=0.5; P(X=1)=0.3P(X = -1) = 0.2; \ P(X=0) = 0.5; \ P(X= 1) = 0.3P(X=−1)=0.2; P(X=0)=0.5; P(X=1)=0.3. Compute E[X2+3]E[X^2 + 3]E[X2+3]

        • We have:

          E[X2+3]=[(−1)2+3]×0.2+(02+3)×0.5+(12+3)×0.3=4×(0.2+0.3)+3×0.5=3.5\begin{aligned} E[X^2 + 3] &= [(-1)^2 + 3] \times 0.2 + (0^2 + 3) \times 0.5 + (1^2 + 3) \times 0.3 \\ &= 4 \times (0.2 + 0.3) + 3 \times 0.5 = 3.5 \end{aligned}E[X2+3]​=[(−1)2+3]×0.2+(02+3)×0.5+(12+3)×0.3=4×(0.2+0.3)+3×0.5=3.5​

  • Continuous Random Variable:

    Given XXX is a continuous random variable having the p.d.f. f(x)f(x)f(x), then, the expected value of XXX is:

    μ=E[X]=∫−∞+∞xf(x) dx\begin{equation} \mu = E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}xf(x) \ dx \end{equation}μ=E[X]=∫−∞+∞​xf(x) dx​​

    which is the balancing point of the p.d.f..

    • LOTUS for continuous r.v.:

      If XXX is a continuous random variable and ggg is a function from R\mathbb{R}R to R\mathbb{R}R, then:

      E[g(X)]=∫−∞+∞g(x)f(x) dx\begin{equation} E[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}g(x)f(x) \ dx \end{equation}E[g(X)]=∫−∞+∞​g(x)f(x) dx​​

Variance

  • Variance summary is the distribution of a random variable, just like expectation. While the expectation shows the center of mass of a distribution, the variance shows how spread out that distribution is. The variance is denoted as Var(X)\text{Var}(X)Var(X).

  • Given the expectation value μ=E[X]\mu = E[X]μ=E[X] of a random variable XXX, the variance is calculated by:

    Var(X)=E[(X−μ)2]=E[X2]−E[X]2\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(X) &= E[(X - \mu)^2]\\ &= E[X^2] - E[X]^2 \end{aligned} \end{equation}Var(X)​=E[(X−μ)2]=E[X2]−E[X]2​​​

  • A useful identity is that for any constant aaa and bbb:

    Var(aX+b)=a2Var(X)\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2\text{Var}(X)Var(aX+b)=a2Var(X)

  • Also, variance is not linear, which means:

    Var(X+Y)≠Var(X)+Var(Y)\text{Var}(X + Y) \neq \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)Var(X+Y)=Var(X)+Var(Y)

  • The square root of the Var(X)\text{Var}(X)Var(X) is called the standard deviation, denoted as SD(X)\text{SD}(X)SD(X) or σ\sigmaσ, of XXX:

    σ=SD(X)=Var(X)=E[(X−μ)2]\sigma = \text{SD}(X) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)} = \sqrt{E[(X - \mu)^2]}σ=SD(X)=Var(X)​=E[(X−μ)2]​

  • Discrete random variable:

    Based on formula (21)(21)(21) of the expected value μ\muμ of a discrete random variable XXX, the variance is calculated by:

    σ2=Var(X)=∑i=1np(xi)⋅(xi−μ)2\begin{equation} \sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^np(x_i)\cdot(x_i - \mu)^2 \end{equation}σ2=Var(X)=i=1∑n​p(xi​)⋅(xi​−μ)2​​

  • Continuous random variable:

    Based on formula (23)(23)(23), with the expected value μ\muμ of a continuous random variable XXX, we have:

    σ2=Var(X)=∫i=1n(xi−μ)2f(x) dx\begin{equation} \sigma^2 = \text{Var}(X) = \int_{i=1}^n(x_i - \mu)^2f(x) \ dx \end{equation}σ2=Var(X)=∫i=1n​(xi​−μ)2f(x) dx​​

    • Example: Find μ\muμ and σ2\sigma^2σ2 of a random variable XXX, given the p.d.f.:

      f(x)={2xif0≤x≤10otherwise\begin{aligned} f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x \quad &\text{if} \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1\\ 0 &\text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned}f(x)={2x0​if0≤x≤1otherwise​​

      We have:

      • Expectation:

        μ=E[X]=∫01xf(x) dx=∫012x2 dx=23\begin{aligned} \mu = E[X]= \int_0^1xf(x) \ dx = \int_0^1 2x^2 \ dx = \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}μ=E[X]=∫01​xf(x) dx=∫01​2x2 dx=32​​

      • Variance:

        E[X2]=∫01x2f(x) dx=∫012x3 dx=12σ2=Var(X)=12−(23)2=118\begin{aligned} E[X^2] &= \int_0^1x^2f(x) \ dx = \int_0^1 2x^3 \ dx = \frac{1}{2}\\ \\ \sigma^2 &= \text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{2} - \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{18} \end{aligned}E[X2]σ2​=∫01​x2f(x) dx=∫01​2x3 dx=21​=Var(X)=21​−(32​)2=181​​

Discrete Probability Distribution

A probability distribution is a statistical model that describes all the probabilities of all possible events that a random variable can take within range. Some distributions are so ubiquitous in probability and statistics that they have their own name.

  • A random variable XXX that is said to have the distribution AAA with parameter ppp is usually denoted as

    X∼A(p)X \sim A(p)X∼A(p)

    which the symbol ∼\sim∼ is read as “is distributed as”

  • Usually, a probability distribution function can be defined by two main parameters: expectation μ\muμ, and variance σ2\sigma^2σ2. But there are cases with different sets of parameters.
  • If XXX follows a certain probability distribution, said Uniform distribution, we can say XXX is a Uniform random variable

Discrete Uniform Distribution

  • Discrete Uniform distribution* is closely connected to the naive definition of probability, described by picking a random number from a finite set of possibilities, where a finite number of values are equally likely to be observed, every one of nnn values has equal probability 1n\frac{1}{n}n1​.

  • Let CCC be a finite, nonempty set of numbers, all values in CCC are equally likely. Choose one of these numbers uniformly at random. The random variable of getting a number XXX follows the Discrete Uniform distribution with parameter CCC, denoted by X∼DUnif(C)X \sim \text{DUnif}(C)X∼DUnif(C). The p.m.f. of the distribution is:

    P(X=x)=1∣C∣\begin{equation} P(X=x) = \frac{1}{|C|} \end{equation}P(X=x)=∣C∣1​​​
  • The examples of the Discrete Uniform Distribution can be rolling a die, tossing a coin (this is a special case of Discrete Uniform Distribution called Bernoulli), or drawing a card from a desk

Bernoulli and Binomial Distribution

  • Bernoulli distribution:

    A discrete random variable XXX is said to have the Bernoulli distribution with parameter ppp if XXX has only two outcomes X=0X = 0X=0 and X=1X = 1X=1, following the probability mass function of X given by:

    {p(1)=P(X=1)=pp(0)=P(X=0)=1−p\begin{equation} \begin{cases} p(1) = P(X=1) = p \\ p(0) = P(X=0) = 1 - p \end{cases} \end{equation}{p(1)=P(X=1)=pp(0)=P(X=0)=1−p​​​

    where 0≤p≤10 \leq p \leq 10≤p≤1 is the probability that the trial success. Therefore, X∼Bern(p)X \sim \text{Bern}(p)X∼Bern(p)

    • The indicator random variable of an event AAA is the random variable which equals 111 if AAA occurs and 000 otherwise. The indicator r.v. of AAA is denoted by IA∼Bern(p)I_A \sim \text{Bern}(p)IA​∼Bern(p) with p=P(A)p = P(A)p=P(A)
    • An experiment where the outcome can be classified as either success or failure is often called a Bernoulli trial. Therefore, ppp is often called the success probability.
    • The most famous example of Bernoulli distribution is the experiment when tossing a single coin. In that case: p=0.5p=0.5p=0.5

  • Binomial distribution:

    Suppose that there are nnn independent Bernoulli trials performed with the same success probability ppp. If discrete random variable XXX represents the number of successes that occur in the nnn trials, then XXX follows the Binomial distribution with parameters nnn and ppp, denoted by X∼Bin(n, p)X \sim \text{Bin}(n, \ p)X∼Bin(n, p), where nnn is a positive number with 0≤p≤10 \leq p \leq 10≤p≤1. Then, the p.m.f. of XXX is:

    p(i)=(ni)pi(1−p)n−iwhere i=0,1,⋯ ,n\begin{equation} p(i) = \begin{pmatrix}n\\ i\end{pmatrix}p^i(1-p)^{n-i} \qquad \text{where } i = 0, 1,\cdots,n \end{equation}p(i)=(ni​)pi(1−p)n−iwhere i=0,1,⋯,n​​

    • By the binomial theorem, all the probabilities sum to 111; that is:

      ∑i=0∞p(i)=∑i=0n(ni)pi(1−p)n−i=[p+(1−p)]n=1\begin{aligned} \sum_{i=0}^\infty p(i) = \sum_{i=0}^n\begin{pmatrix}n\\ i\end{pmatrix}p^i(1-p)^{n-i} = [p + (1 - p)]^n = 1 \end{aligned}i=0∑∞​p(i)=i=0∑n​(ni​)pi(1−p)n−i=[p+(1−p)]n=1​
    • Example: A pack of screws produced by a certain company will be defective with a probability 0.020.020.02, independently for each screw. The company sells the screws in packages of 10 and offers a money-back guarantee that at most 111 of 101010 screws are defective. What proportion of packages sold must the company replace?

      • If XXX is the number of defective screws in a package, then X∼Bin(10, 0.02)X \sim \text{Bin}(10, \ 0.02)X∼Bin(10, 0.02). Hence, the probability that a package will have to be replaced is:

        1−p(0)−p1)=1−(100)(0.02)0(0.98)10−(101)(0.02)1(0.98)9≈0.0161 -p(0) - p_1) = 1 - \begin{pmatrix}10\\ 0\end{pmatrix}(0.02)^0 (0.98)^10 - \begin{pmatrix}10\\ 1\end{pmatrix}(0.02)^1 (0.98)^9 \approx 0.0161−p(0)−p1​)=1−(100​)(0.02)0(0.98)10−(101​)(0.02)1(0.98)9≈0.016

    • Expectation and variance:

      If X∼Bin(n, p)X \sim \text{Bin}(n, \ p)X∼Bin(n, p), then:

      E[X]=npVar(x)=np(1−p)\begin{aligned} E[X] &= np\\ \text{Var}(x) &= np(1-p) \end{aligned}E[X]Var(x)​=np=np(1−p)​
    • Several Binomial p.m.f., given different sets of (n,p)(n, p)(n,p):

Categorical and Multipomial Distribution

  • Categorical distribution:

    Categorical distribution is the generalization of Bernoulli distribution. Thus, sometimes it can be referred as a generalized Bernoulli distribution or multinoulli distribution.

    Given XXX is a discrete random variable that follows the Categorical distribution, XXX then takes on one of X∼Catk(k, p)X\sim \text{Cat}_k(k,\ \boldsymbol{p})X∼Catk​(k, p) possible categories, with the probability of each category separately specified. Therefore: X∼Catk(k, p)X\sim \text{Cat}_k(k,\ \boldsymbol{p})X∼Catk​(k, p) where p=[p1,⋯ , pk]\boldsymbol{p} = [p_1,\cdots, \ p_k]p=[p1​,⋯, pk​].

    • Note that all the probability p1,⋯ , pkp_1, \cdots, \ p_kp1​,⋯, pk​ must be in the range of 000 and 111, and all must sum 111.

    • The probability mass function of Categorical distribution is:

      p(i∣p)=∏i=1kpi[x=i]\begin{equation} p(i|\boldsymbol{p}) = \prod_{i=1}^kp_i^{[x = i]} \end{equation}p(i∣p)=i=1∏k​pi[x=i]​​​

      where [x=i][x = i][x=i] is in the Inverson bracket, indicates that:

      [x=i]={1if x=i0otherwise[x=i] = \begin{cases} 1 \quad & \text{if } x=i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}[x=i]={10​if x=iotherwise​
    • A well-known example of the categorical distribution is the experiment that rolls a fair die, where each p(i)=16p(i) = \frac{1}{6}p(i)=61​

  • Multinomial distribution:

    It is the generalization of Binomial distribution, where multiple independent trials follow the Categorical distribution. Given XXX is a discrete random variable that follows the Multipomial distribution consisting of nnn Categorical trials that outcome kkk results independently, we denote X∼Multk(n, p)X \sim \text{Mult}_k(n, \ \boldsymbol{p})X∼Multk​(n, p), with the p.m.f.:

    P(X1=n1, X2=n2,⋯ , Xk=nk)=n!n1!n2!⋯nk!p1n1p2n2⋯pknk\begin{equation} P(X_1=n_1,\ X_2=n_2,\cdots,\ X_k = n_k)= \frac{n!}{n_1!n2!\cdots n_k!}p_1^{n_1}p_2^{n_2}\cdots p_k^{n_k} \end{equation}P(X1​=n1​, X2​=n2​,⋯, Xk​=nk​)=n1​!n2!⋯nk​!n!​p1n1​​p2n2​​⋯pknk​​​​

    whenever ∑i=1kni=n\begin{aligned}\sum_{i=1}^kn_i =n\end{aligned}i=1∑k​ni​=n​, with nin_ini​ is the number that result iii-th happened.

    • Example: Suppose 9 fair dice are rolled. The probability that 111 appears three times, 222 and 333 appear twice each, 444 and 555 once each, and 666 not at all is:

      9!3!2!2!1!1!0!(16)3(16)2(16)2(16)1(16)1(16)0=9!3!2!2!(16)9=1419904\begin{aligned} \frac{9!}{3!2!2!1!1!0!}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^3\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^2\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^2\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^1\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^1\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^0 = \frac{9!}{3!2!2!}\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)^9 = \frac{1}{419904} \end{aligned}3!2!2!1!1!0!9!​(61​)3(61​)2(61​)2(61​)1(61​)1(61​)0=3!2!2!9!​(61​)9=4199041​​

Hypergeometric Distribution

  • Suppose that a sample of size nnn is to be chosen randomly without replacement from an urn containing of mmm white balls and bbb black balls. If we let XXX denote the number of white balls selected, then XXX is said to have the Hergeometric distribution with parameters (w,b,n)(w, b, n)(w,b,n), denoted by X∼HGeom(m, b, n)X \sim \text{HGeom}(m,\ b,\ n)X∼HGeom(m, b, n). The p.m.f. of XXX is:

    P(X=k)=(mk)(bn−k)(w+bn)\begin{equation} P(X = k) = \frac{\begin{pmatrix}m\\k\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}b\\n-k\end{pmatrix}}{\begin{pmatrix}w+b\\n\end{pmatrix}} \end{equation}P(X=k)=(w+bn​)(mk​)(bn−k​)​​​

  • Example: A purchaser of electrical components buys them in lots of size 101010. It is his policy to inspect 333 components randomly from a lot and to accept the lot only if all 333 are non-defective. If 30%30\%30% of the lots have 444 defective components and 70%70\%70% have only 1, what proportion of lots does the purchaser reject?

    • Let AAA denote the event that the purchaser accepts a lot. Now we have:

      P(A)=P(A∣lot have 4 defectives)310+P(A∣lot have 1 defectives)710=(40)(63)(103)310+(10)(93)(103)710=54100\begin{aligned} P(A) &= P(A|\text{lot have 4 defectives})\frac{3}{10} + P(A|\text{lot have 1 defectives})\frac{7}{10}\\ &= \frac{\begin{pmatrix}4\\0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}6\\3\end{pmatrix}}{\begin{pmatrix}10\\3\end{pmatrix}}\frac{3}{10} + \frac{\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}9\\3\end{pmatrix}}{\begin{pmatrix}10\\3\end{pmatrix}}\frac{7}{10} = \frac{54}{100} \end{aligned}P(A)​=P(A∣lot have 4 defectives)103​+P(A∣lot have 1 defectives)107​=(103​)(40​)(63​)​103​+(103​)(10​)(93​)​107​=10054​​

Poisson Distribution

  • A discrete random variable has the Poisson distribution with parameter λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0, denoted by X∼Pois(λ)X \sim \text{Pois}(\lambda)X∼Pois(λ), if the p.m.f. of XXX is:

    p(k)=e−kλkk!\begin{equation} p(k) = \frac{e^{-k}\lambda^k}{k!} \end{equation}p(k)=k!e−kλk​​​

    • We have the expectation and variance of the Poisson distribution are both equal to λ\lambdaλ:

      Expectation:E[X]=e−λ∑k=0∞kλkk!=e−λ∑k=1∞kλkk!=λe−λ∑k=1∞kλk−1(k−1)!=λe−λeλ=λVariance:E[X2]=e−λ∑k=0∞k2λkk!=e−λeλλ(1+λ)=λ(1+λ)⇒Var(x)=E[X2]−E[X]2=λ(1+λ)−λ2=λ\begin{aligned} \text{Expectation:} \qquad E[X] &= e^{-\lambda}\sum_{k=0}^\infty k\frac{\lambda^k}{k!} = e^{-\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^\infty k\frac{\lambda^k}{k!}\\ &= \lambda e^{-\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^\infty k\frac{\lambda^{k-1}}{(k-1)!} = \lambda e^{-\lambda}e^\lambda = \lambda \\ \text{Variance:} \qquad E[X^2] &= e^{-\lambda}\sum_{k=0}^\infty k^2\frac{\lambda^k}{k!} = e^{-\lambda}e^\lambda\lambda(1 +\lambda) = \lambda(1 + \lambda)\\ \Rightarrow \quad \text{Var}(x) &= E[X^2] - E[X]^2 = \lambda(1 + \lambda) - \lambda^2 = \lambda \end{aligned}Expectation:E[X]Variance:E[X2]⇒Var(x)​=e−λk=0∑∞​kk!λk​=e−λk=1∑∞​kk!λk​=λe−λk=1∑∞​k(k−1)!λk−1​=λe−λeλ=λ=e−λk=0∑∞​k2k!λk​=e−λeλλ(1+λ)=λ(1+λ)=E[X2]−E[X]2=λ(1+λ)−λ2=λ​
  • The Poisson distribution is often used in situations when counting the number of successes in a particular region or interval of time, or there are a large number of trials, each with a small probability of success.
    • For example:
      • The number of emails you received in an hour. Imagine, there are 3.6×1063.6 \times 10^63.6×106 seconds in an hour, but in any specific millisecond, it is unlikely that you will get an email.
      • The number of chips in a chocolate chip cookie, if we divide the cookie into small cubes; where the number of cubes is large, but the probability of getting a chocolate chip inside a single cube is small.
      • Then, the parameter λ\lambdaλ is interpreted as the rate of occurrence of these rare events. In these examples, λ\lambdaλ could be 202020 emails per hour or 101010 chips per cookie
    • In general, the Poisson distribution may be used as an approximation for a Binomial distribution with parameters (n,p)(n, p)(n,p) where nnn is large and ppp is small enough so that npnpnp is of moderate size, by letting λ=np\lambda = npλ=np

      • Specifically: if X∼Bin(n, p)X \sim \text{Bin}(n, \ p)X∼Bin(n, p) and we let n→∞n \rightarrow \inftyn→∞ and p→0p \rightarrow 0p→0 such that λ=np\lambda = npλ=np remains fixed, then the p.m.f. of XXX converges to the Pois(λ)\text{Pois}(\lambda)Pois(λ) p.m.f.. Therefore:

        P(X=k)→e−λλkk!P(X = k) \rightarrow \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}P(X=k)→k!e−λλk​
  • Examples:
    • Example 1: Suppose that the number of typographical errors on a single page of this book has a Poisson distribution with parameter λ=12\lambda = \frac{1}{2}λ=21​. Calculate the probability that there is at least one error on this page:

      • Let XXX denote the number of errors on this page, we have:

        P(X≥1)=1−P(X=0)=1−e121200!≈0.393\begin{aligned} P(X \geq 1) = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - e ^\frac{1}{2}\frac{\frac{1}{2}^0}{0!} \approx 0.393 \end{aligned}P(X≥1)=1−P(X=0)=1−e21​0!21​0​≈0.393​
    • Example 2: At least how many people are required in order to have a 50-50 chance that two people would have birthdays within one day of each other?
      • The probability that any two people have birthdays within one day of each other is 3365\begin{aligned}\frac{3}{365}\end{aligned}3653​​ (choose a birthday for the first person, then the second person needs to be born on that day, the day before, or the day after)
      • There are (m2)\begin{pmatrix}m \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}(m2​) possible pairs, so the number within-one-day matches is approximately Pois(λ)\text{Pois}(\lambda)Pois(λ) where (m2)3365\begin{pmatrix}m \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\frac{3}{365}(m2​)3653​.

      • Therefore, to have a 50-50 chance of having a within-one-day birthday, we have:

        P(X≥1)=1−P(X=0)=1−e≈0.5⇒e−(m2)3365≈0.5⇒m≈14\begin{aligned} &P(X \geq 1) = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - e^{}\approx 0.5 \\ &\Rightarrow e^{-\begin{pmatrix}m \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\frac{3}{365}} \approx 0.5 \Rightarrow m \approx 14 \end{aligned}​P(X≥1)=1−P(X=0)=1−e≈0.5⇒e−(m2​)3653​≈0.5⇒m≈14​

Continuous Probability Distribution

Continuous Uniform Distribution

  • Similar to the discrete case, a continuous random variable XXX follows the continuous Uniform Distribution on the interval (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), denoted by X∼Unif(a,b)X \sim \text{Unif}(a,b)X∼Unif(a,b), is a completely random number between aaa and bbb, if its p.d.f. is:

    f(x)={1b−aif x∈[a, b]0otherwise\begin{equation} f(x) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{b-a} \qquad &\text{if } x \in [a,\ b]\\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{equation}f(x)={b−a1​0​if x∈[a, b]otherwise​​​

    • The c.d.f. of the distribution í the accumulated area under the p.d.f., which is

      F(x)={0if x≤ax−an−aif x∈[a, b]1if x≥bF(x) = \begin{cases}0 &\text{if } x \leq a \\ \frac{x-a}{n-a} \qquad &\text{if } x \in [a, \ b] \\ 1 &\text{if } x \geq b \end{cases}F(x)=⎩⎨⎧​0n−ax−a​1​if x≤aif x∈[a, b]if x≥b​

  • A good example of uniform distribution is an idealized random number generator. However, as every variable has an equal chance of happening, there is an infinity number of points that can exist, even in a small range.

Gaussian Distribution

  • Gaussian Distribution (or Normal Distribution) is the most famous distribution and is extremely widely used in statistics
    • The Central Limit Theorem says that, under very weak assumptions, the sum of a large number of independent and identically distributed random variables (discrete or continuous) has an approximately Normal distribution
      • Moreover, in practice, many random phenomena obey, at least approximately, a normal distribution

  • Standard Normal Distribution: A continuous random variable ZZZ is said to have the standard Normal Distribution Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)Z∼N(0,1) if its p.d.f. is given by:

    φ(z)=12πe−z2/2\begin{align} \varphi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2} \end{align}φ(z)=2π​1​e−z2/2​​

    • The c.d.f. of a Normal Distribution, often denoted as Φ\PhiΦ, is the accumulated area under the p.d.f., calculated by:

      Φ(z)=∫−∞zφ(t) dt=∫−∞z12πe−t2/2dt\begin{equation} \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^z\varphi(t)\ dt = \int_{-\infty}^z\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2/2} dt \end{equation}Φ(z)=∫−∞z​φ(t) dt=∫−∞z​2π​1​e−t2/2dt​​

    • Expectation and Variance:

      E[X]=12π∫−∞+∞ze−z2/2 dz=−12πe−z2/2∣−∞+∞=0\begin{aligned} E[X] &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}ze^{-z^2/2} \ dz \\ & = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\big|_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\\ &= 0 \end{aligned}E[X]​=2π​1​∫−∞+∞​ze−z2/2 dz=−2π​1​e−z2/2∣∣​−∞+∞​=0​ Var(Z)=E[Z2]−E[Z]2=22π∫0+∞z2e−z2/2 dx=22π(−ze−z2/2∣0∞+∫0∞e−z2/2 dz)=22π(0+2π2)=1\begin{aligned} \text{Var}(Z) &= E[Z^2] - E[Z]^2\\ &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{0}^{+\infty}z^2e^{-z^2/2} \ dx \\ &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(-ze^{-z^2/2}\bigg|_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-z^2/2} \ dz \right) \\ &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\left(0 + \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{2}\right) = 1 \end{aligned}Var(Z)​=E[Z2]−E[Z]2=2π​2​∫0+∞​z2e−z2/2 dx=2π​2​(−ze−z2/2∣∣​0∞​+∫0∞​e−z2/2 dz)=2π​2​(0+22π​​)=1​

  • Normal Distribution with variance and expectation: If Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)Z∼N(0,1), a continuous random variable X=μ+σZX = \mu + \sigma ZX=μ+σZ is said to follow the Normal Distribution with expectation μ\muμ and variance σ2\sigma^2σ2 , denoted by X∼N(μ, σ2)X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\ \sigma^2)X∼N(μ, σ2). Thus, its p.d.f. is given by:

    f(x)=φ(x−μσ)1σ=12πσ2e−(x−μ)2/2σ2\begin{equation} f(x) = \varphi \left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)\frac{1}{\sigma}= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}e^{-(x - \mu)^2/2\sigma^2} \end{equation}f(x)=φ(σx−μ​)σ1​=2πσ2​1​e−(x−μ)2/2σ2​​

    • The 68-95-99.7% rule: If X∼N(μ, σ2)X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \ \sigma^2)X∼N(μ, σ2), then:
    P(∣X−μ∣<σ)≈0.68P(∣X−μ∣<2σ)≈0.95P(∣X−μ∣<3σ)≈0.997\begin{aligned} P(|X - \mu| < \sigma) &\approx 0.68\\ P(|X - \mu| < 2\sigma) &\approx 0.95\\ P(|X - \mu| < 3\sigma) &\approx 0.997\\ \end{aligned}P(∣X−μ∣<σ)P(∣X−μ∣<2σ)P(∣X−μ∣<3σ)​≈0.68≈0.95≈0.997​
  • The Normal approximation to the Binomial Distribution:
    • When nnn large, a binomial random variable with parameters nnn and ppp will approximately follow the same distribution as a normal random variable with the same mean μ=np\mu = npμ=np and variance σ2=np(1−p)\sigma^2 = np(1-p)σ2=np(1−p) ** as the binomial (this is one case of the Central Limit Theorem)

    • The DeMoivre-Laplace limit theorem: If SNS_NSN​ denotes the number of successes that occur when nnn independent trials perform, each resulting with probability ppp, then, for any a<ba < ba<b:

      P(a≤Sn−npnp(1−p)≤b)→Φ(b)−Φ(a)asn→∞\begin{aligned} P\left(a \leq \frac{S_n - np}{\sqrt{}np(1-p)}\leq b \right) \rightarrow \Phi(b) - \Phi(a)\\ \end{aligned} \quad \text{as} \quad n \rightarrow \inftyP(a≤​np(1−p)Sn​−np​≤b)→Φ(b)−Φ(a)​asn→∞

      • One example to visualize the theorem is the Galton Board, which can be observed by this GIF:

    • Example: Let XXX be the number of times that a fair coin that is flipped 40 times lands on heads. Find the probability that X=20X=20X=20 using the normal approximation and then compare it with the extract solution

      • Because the binomials is a discrete integer-valued random variable, whereas the normal is a continuous random variable, it is best to write P(X=i)P(X = i)P(X=i) as P(i−1/2<X<i+1/2)P(i - 1/2 < X < i + 1/2)P(i−1/2<X<i+1/2) before applying the normal approximation. Doing so gives:

        P(X=20)=P(19.5≤X≤20.5)=P(19.5−2010<X−2010<20−2010)≈P(−0.16<X−2010<0.16)≈Θ(0.16)−Θ(−0.16)≈0.1272\begin{aligned} P(X=20) &= P(19.5 \leq X \leq 20.5)\\ &= P\left(\frac{19.5 - 20}{\sqrt{10}}<\frac{X - 20}{\sqrt{10}}<\frac{20 - 20}{\sqrt{10}}\right)\\ &\approx P\left(-0.16<\frac{X - 20}{\sqrt{10}}<0.16\right)\\ &\approx \Theta(0.16) - \Theta(-0.16) \approx0.1272 \end{aligned}P(X=20)​=P(19.5≤X≤20.5)=P(10​19.5−20​<10​X−20​<10​20−20​)≈P(−0.16<10​X−20​<0.16)≈Θ(0.16)−Θ(−0.16)≈0.1272​

        On the other hand, the extract result is:

        P(X=20)=(4020)(12)40≈0.1254\begin{aligned}P(X=20)=\begin{pmatrix}40\\20\end{pmatrix}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{40}\approx 0.1254\end{aligned}P(X=20)=(4020​)(21​)40≈0.1254​

Exponential Distribution

  • The Exponential Distribution often arises as the distribution of the amount of time until some specific event occurs. For instance, the amount of time (starting from now) until an earthquake occurs, or until a new war breaks out, ect.

  • A continuous random variable X follows the Exponential Distribution X∼Expo(λ)X \sim \text{Expo}(\lambda)X∼Expo(λ) with parameter λ>0\lambda >0λ>0 if its p.d.f. is:

    f(x)=λe−λxx>0\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \qquad x > 0 \end{aligned} \end{equation}f(x)=λe−λxx>0​​​

  • The cossesponding c.d.f. is:

    F(x)=1−e−λxx>0F(x)= 1 - e^{-\lambda x} \qquad x > 0F(x)=1−e−λxx>0

  • Expectation and variance:

    E[X]=1λ;Var(X)=1λ2\begin{aligned} E[X] = \frac{1}{\lambda};\quad\qquad \text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \end{aligned}E[X]=λ1​;Var(X)=λ21​​

  • Example: Suppose that a length of a phone call in minutes is an exponential random variable with λ=110\lambda = \frac{1}{10}λ=101​. If someone arrives immediately ahead of you at a public telephone booth, find the probability that you have to wait: (a) more than 10 minutes; (b) between 10 and 20 minutes:

    • Let XXX denote the length of the call made by the person in the booth, we have:

      P(X>10)=1−F(10)=e−1≈0.368P(X > 10) = 1 - F(10) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368P(X>10)=1−F(10)=e−1≈0.368 P(10<X<20)=F(20)−F(10)=e−1−e−2≈0.233P(10 < X<20) = F(20) - F(10) = e^{-1} - e^{-2} \approx 0.233P(10<X<20)=F(20)−F(10)=e−1−e−2≈0.233

Gamma Distribution

  • A random variable XXX is said to have Gamma distribution with parameter α>0; β>0\alpha > 0; \ \beta > 0α>0; β>0, denoted as X∼Γ(α;β)X \sim \Gamma(\alpha; \beta)X∼Γ(α;β) if its p.d.f. is given by:

    f(x;α,β)=βe−βx(βx)α−1Γ(α)x>0\begin{equation} f(x; \alpha, \beta) = \frac{\beta e^{-\beta x}(\beta x)^{\alpha - 1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \qquad x > 0 \end{equation}f(x;α,β)=Γ(α)βe−βx(βx)α−1​x>0​​

    where Γ(α)\Gamma(\alpha)Γ(α), called the gamma function, is calculated by:

    • For integral values α\alphaα (discrete):

      Γ(α)=(α−1)!\Gamma(\alpha) = (\alpha - 1)!Γ(α)=(α−1)!

    • For real values α\alphaα (continuous):

      Γ(α)=∫0∞tα−1e−t dt\begin{aligned}\Gamma(\alpha) = \int_0^\infty t^{\alpha - 1}e^{-t} \ dt \end{aligned}Γ(α)=∫0∞​tα−1e−t dt​

  • Expectation and variance:

    E[X]=αβ;Var(X)=αβ2\begin{aligned} E[X] = \frac{\alpha}{\beta};\quad\qquad \text{Var}(X) = \frac{\alpha}{\beta^2} \end{aligned}E[X]=βα​;Var(X)=β2α​​

Beta Distribution

  • A random variable XXX is said to have Beta distribution with parameter α>0; β>0\alpha > 0; \ \beta > 0α>0; β>0, denoted as X∼B(α;β)X \sim \Beta(\alpha; \beta)X∼B(α;β) if its p.d.f. is given by:

    f(x;α,β)=1B(α, β)xα−1(1−x)β−10≤x≤1\begin{equation} f(x; \alpha, \beta) =\frac{1}{B(\alpha,\ \beta)}x^{\alpha - 1}(1-x)^{\beta - 1} \qquad 0 \leq x \leq 1 \end{equation}f(x;α,β)=B(α, β)1​xα−1(1−x)β−10≤x≤1​​

    where B(α,β)\Beta(\alpha, \beta)B(α,β), called the beta function, is a normalization constant to ensure that the total probability is 111, calculated by:

    B(α,β)=Γ(α)Γ(β)Γ(α+β)=∫01xα−1(1−x)β−1 dx\begin{aligned} B(\alpha, \beta) = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)} = \int_0^1x^{\alpha - 1}(1 - x)^{\beta - 1}\ dx \end{aligned}B(α,β)=Γ(α+β)Γ(α)Γ(β)​=∫01​xα−1(1−x)β−1 dx​

  • Expectation and variance:

    E[X]=αα+β;Var(X)=αβ(α+β)2(α+β+1)\begin{aligned} E[X] = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta};\quad\qquad \text{Var}(X) = \frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)} \end{aligned}E[X]=α+βα​;Var(X)=(α+β)2(α+β+1)αβ​​

Recap of basic probability

Four fundamental concepts in probability

  • The connections between four fundamental concepts in probability:
    • From the distribution, we can obtain the distribution function c.d.f. FFF
    • From the c.d.f., we can generate the random variables XXX, or other random variables by taking the function of XXX.
    • There are various events xxx describing the behavior of XXX. We mostly interest in the event X≤xX \leq xX≤x and X=xX = xX=x.
    • Then, we can calculate the value number of the probability of these events, by Bayesian rules for example.
    • Knowing the probability of these events for all xxx gives us the c.d.f. and the p.m.f./p.d.f. of the random variable XXX, thus describing the distribution of the random variable.
